```python

In [135]: service_is_up("https://www.amazon.com")
```

```python
In [136]: requests.get("https://www.amazon.com")
Out[136]: <Response [503]>
# Ah, Amazon specifically excludes requests UA (https://requestb.in/17aaf8o1?inspect)
```

I believe that the response should be released manually if it's not read or used in a context manager. So there are (at least) a couple of options, and I'm not sure if one is more efficient than the other.
```python
resp = await session.get('https://sso.pokemon.com/sso/logout', params={'service': 'https%3A%2F%2Fsso.pokemon.com%2Fsso%2Foauth2.0%2FcallbackAuthorize'}, proxy=self.proxy, proxy_auth=self.proxy_auth, allow_redirects=False)
resp.release()
```
 or
```python
async with session.get('https://sso.pokemon.com/sso/logout', params={'service': 'https%3A%2F%2Fsso.pokemon.com%2Fsso%2Foauth2.0%2FcallbackAuthorize'}, proxy=self.proxy, proxy_auth=self.proxy_auth, allow_redirects=False) as _:
    pass
```

As I understand it, the original protocol sends out a query  bytearray([0xef, 0x01, 0x77]) and expects 11 bytes as a response.
the newer protocols all send out a query bytearray([0x81, 0x8a, 0x8b]) and expects 14 bytes as a response. 

if you don't specify that the original protocol should be used, the default (new) bytearray is sent and a new 14 byte response is expected.

> Actually my codes implement an "always-bytes protocol". On Python 2, sys.prefix is a bytes-like object, so I print it as-is.

But strings in Python 2 use ```sys.getdefaltencoding()``` as the encoding, not ```sys.getfilesystemencoding()```, so what you're writing is going to have a different encoding depending on the Python version (unless the default and filesystem encodings are the same, which isn't always the case - and on Windows there's also the OEM encoding to make things even more messy).

A "strings encoded in UTF-8" protocol is far more robust. If you've actually found a case where that protocol doesn't work, you don't make it clear what that case is, so I don't see the value in this change.

At the absolute minimum, this code needs a comment explaining what is wrong with the original approach (of passing string values between subprocess and main program using a known encoding) and how the modified version solves that issue. Ideally, there should be a test, but the virtualenv test suite doesn't really go down to this level :-(

Also, you're making the assumption that ```sys.getfilesystemencoding()``` is the same in both the subprocess Python and the current Python. While that's not likely to be violated, I don't see any benefit in taking that risk, when a specific UTF-8 encoding is just as good for converting arbitrary string data to a binary stream, and involves no such risk.

After reading your comment and giving some more thought to the actual purpose of this class, I decided to refactor it slightly.  Essentially the purpose of a SpecialIndex in this library is for use by the database API method `get_all_indexes()`.  'get_all_indexes()` retrieves the indexes in the database through the _index endpoint and wraps the primary "special" index as a SpecialIndex object as well as wrapping JSON indexes as Index objects and TEXT indexes as SearchIndex objects and returns the index list to the caller.

Since you cannot really do anything with a SpecialIndex object other than instanciating it, it didn't really make much sense to have any kind of validation as was originally being done.  So the validation was removed and the method signature was changed to match that if the parent Index class.  This change provides us with flexibility if we (Cloudant) decide to change the "special" index definition server side at any point down the road (unlikely but still possible), this library implementation will still be able to handle it.

So my suggestion was not focused on VM semantics, but rather our python API semantics.  Here's the situation I'm trying to avoid.

```python
# somewhere we set the gas price.
msg.paygas_gasprice = 10

# elsewhere in the code, we try to set it again.
msg.paygas_price = 100  # this will silently fail
```

What I would like is that the later case cannot silently fail, hence, suggesting that we raise an exception.  Then, if we ever have a location in the code which tries to set the gas price when it's already set which doesn't explicitely handle whatever exception we raise, then we'll get a hard failure in our code, rather than a silent failure that only manifests later at some point when the gas accounting is off.

Yeah, gcloud is the standard tool (similar to aws-cli) that the user would have  installed on their local machine.

https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/compute/ssh:
> gcloud compute ssh is a thin wrapper around the ssh(1) command that takes care of authentication and the translation of the instance name into an IP address.

Basically you can ssh to instances using the instance names and, if I understand correctly, gcloud will handle the required keys to the machine (assuming you have your gcloud authenticated):
```bash
gcloud compute ssh --project=<project-name>@ray-<cluster-name>-head
```

The current implementation sets up a project wide ssh key that is used by both the service account and the user, and by default grants access to all instances created in the project. We could change the ssh key flow such that we [generate specific ssh key for the service account](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/creating-managing-service-account-keys) and then the user would have to either create their own project wide keys or use `gcloud compute ssh` to access the nodes from their local machine.

I think splitting the ssh keys could be cleaner from user's perspective. One thing to note is that the service account keys have default expiration time (10 days iirc?) which currently can't be changed.

Two choices here:
 - Let event types have more control over their state transition logic (probably)
 - Write the state machine to handle `REVISED` or `CREATED` everywhere.

I think the versioning logic allows it but the transitioning logic might not.

 - The first version is always 1
 - If the first event were of a restarting type, it would still start at version 1
 - If the restarting type came later, you'd get version N+1

I don't think it's easy to express "initial or [some other condition]" yet as a state transition.

This was a bit of pre-planning for making GalSim compatible with [Python 3](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/issues/534).  The `//` operator has the same meaning in both Python 2 and 3.  But `/` does not.

Ideally, I'd like to never use `from __future__ import division` if possible, since I don't actually like having integer division return a float as it does in Python 3.  That runs against all my instincts as a C/C++ programmer.  `5 / 3` is 1, not 1.66666667.   But writing `//` at least makes me more cognizant of the issue without any ambiguity.

We would either need to subclass to test it, or else allow tests to inject a value which overrides the pure virtualness.

The reason why: The entire "right thing" happens with the `==` operator. So, `self.__eq__(other)` gets called, _but_ unlike if we returned `not self.__eq__(other)` directly, if it returns `NotImplemented`, then the other object is asked and it is completely resolved to the boolean, which is then inverted.

Therefore, while `__ne__` never returns `NotImplemented`, it does correctly resolve the one that `__eq__` returns.
